name: Beryllium
name_origin: From the mineral beryl (Greek 'beryllos'), in which the element was first identified by Louis-Nicolas Vauquelin.

discovery.discoverer: Louis-Nicolas Vauquelin (identified); Friedrich Wohler and Antoine Bussy isolated the metal in 1828

occurrence.abundance_summary: Beryllium is relatively rare in Earth's crust (~2.8 ppm) and never found as the free element. The principal ores are beryl (Be3Al2Si6O18) and bertrandite; gem-quality beryl varieties include emerald and aquamarine.
states: trace component in many silicate minerals

compounds.name: Beryllium oxide
compounds.summary: A high-melting ceramic with exceptional thermal conductivity, used in electronics and nuclear reactors but toxic if inhaled as dust.

compounds.name: Beryllium fluoride
compounds.summary: A glassy ionic solid used in molten-salt nuclear reactor coolants (FLiBe).

compounds.name: Beryllium hydroxide
compounds.summary: An amphoteric hydroxide that is an intermediate in extracting beryllium from ores.

compounds.name: Beryllium chloride
compounds.summary: A covalent, polymeric solid used as a Lewis acid catalyst and a precursor to beryllium metal.

compounds.name: Beryl
compounds.summary: The principal beryllium-bearing mineral; gem-quality forms include emerald (green) and aquamarine (blue).

uses: Beryllium-copper alloys for non-sparking, non-magnetic precision tools and springs
X-ray window material due to its low atomic number and low X-ray absorption
Structural components in aerospace and satellites (high stiffness-to-weight ratio)
Neutron reflector and moderator in nuclear reactors and weapons
Mirror substrates for the James Webb Space Telescope and other precision optics
Beryllium oxide ceramic substrates in high-power RF electronics
reactivity_summary: Beryllium is a hard, lightweight, steel-gray metal that forms a passivating oxide layer giving good corrosion resistance. It is amphoteric, dissolving in both acids and strong alkalis. Inhaled beryllium dust and compounds are highly toxic, causing chronic beryllium disease (berylliosis), so industrial handling is tightly regulated.
isotopes_summary: Only one stable isotope, 9Be, exists; cosmogenic 10Be (half-life ~1.4 million years) is produced by cosmic-ray spallation and is widely used in geological dating of ice cores and marine sediments.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Beryllium
